title: Transmission-Based (Isolation) Precautions Across a Multi-Patient Assignment: Correctly Matching CONTACT vs DROPLET vs AIRBORNE vs PROTECTIVE Environment, Choosing the Right Room (AIIR Negative-Pressure vs Positive-Pressure) and PPE (N95 vs Surgical Mask), Soap-and-Water for C. difficile Spores, and the Safe Donning/Doffing Sequence
subtitle: STANDARD PRECAUTIONS apply to EVERY patient regardless of diagnosis (hand hygiene; PPE based on the anticipated exposure to blood/body fluids; respiratory hygiene/cough etiquette; safe injection practices; clean equipment/environment) and are the foundation. TRANSMISSION-BASED PRECAUTIONS are added when a specific organism/route is suspected or confirmed, and are layered on top of standard precautions. CONTACT precautions (gown + gloves, private room or cohorting, DEDICATED/disposable equipment) are used for organisms spread by direct/indirect contact: MULTIDRUG-RESISTANT ORGANISMS (MRSA, VRE, CRE/ESBL), Clostridioides difficile, norovirus, scabies/lice, RSV, and major draining wounds. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: for C. DIFFICILE, NOROVIRUS, and SCABIES, hand hygiene must be SOAP AND WATER (mechanical removal) because ALCOHOL-BASED sanitizer does NOT kill C. diff SPORES, and the room is cleaned with a SPORICIDAL agent (bleach). DROPLET precautions (a SURGICAL/procedure mask within ~3-6 feet, private room or cohorting; the patient wears a surgical mask during transport) are used for large respiratory droplets: INFLUENZA, PERTUSSIS (whooping cough), Neisseria MENINGITIDIS (meningococcal disease), Haemophilus influenzae type b, MUMPS, RUBELLA, and group A streptococcal pharyngitis. AIRBORNE precautions (a fit-tested N95 RESPIRATOR or PAPR; an AIRBORNE-INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM [AIIR] with NEGATIVE PRESSURE, ≥6-12 air changes/hour, and the DOOR KEPT CLOSED; the patient wears a SURGICAL mask during transport) are used for tiny airborne nuclei that stay suspended: tuberculosis (TB), MEASLES (rubeola), VARICELLA (chickenpox), and disseminated herpes ZOSTER ('My Chicken Hez TB' = Measles, Chickenpox/varicella, Herpes zoster disseminated, TB). For measles/varicella, SUSCEPTIBLE (nonimmune) staff should ideally NOT enter; immune staff are assigned. A PROTECTIVE ENVIRONMENT (a.k.a. reverse/neutropenic isolation) is the OPPOSITE goal — it protects a SEVERELY IMMUNOCOMPROMISED patient (e.g., the neutropenic chemo or hematopoietic-stem-cell-transplant patient) FROM the environment: a POSITIVE-PRESSURE room with HEPA filtration and high air exchange, the door closed, NO fresh flowers/potted plants or standing water (fungal/Pseudomonas risk), only healthy visitors, and minimizing exposure; it is NOT negative pressure (that would be backwards). When a patient has more than one route (e.g., varicella = airborne AND contact; some MDRO pneumonias), COMBINE the precautions. PPE SEQUENCE matters: DON in the order GOWN → MASK/RESPIRATOR → GOGGLES/face shield → GLOVES (before entering); DOFF in the order GLOVES → GOGGLES/face shield → GOWN → MASK/RESPIRATOR (remove the most contaminated item first; remove the respirator LAST, outside the room/after the door is closed), with HAND HYGIENE after removal (and between steps as needed). Other principles: COHORT like-with-like when private rooms are scarce; use DEDICATED equipment (or disinfect between patients); post the correct SIGNAGE and stock PPE at the door; limit/clear transport and have the patient masked; and educate the patient, family, and visitors without stigmatizing them. NURSING ROLE: recognize the suspected organism/route from the cues; SELECT and INITIATE the correct transmission-based precautions (don't wait for culture confirmation if clinically suspected); choose the right ROOM (negative-pressure AIIR for airborne, positive-pressure for protective) and the right PPE (N95 for airborne, surgical mask for droplet); use SOAP-AND-WATER + bleach for C. diff/norovirus/spores; assign immune staff for measles/varicella; don/doff PPE correctly; cohort and use dedicated equipment when needed; and teach the patient/family/visitors.

patient.name: Multiple patients (isolation assignment)
patient.code: Goal = correctly match each patient to the right transmission-based precaution, room (negative- vs positive-pressure), and PPE; use soap-and-water/bleach for C. difficile; assign immune staff for measles; and don/doff PPE safely
patient.admitDate: A medical-surgical/step-down RN is receiving several patients at once and must rapidly assign the CORRECT isolation precautions, room, and PPE for each: (A) a man with cough × 4 weeks, night sweats, weight loss, and a CAVITARY lesion with sputum AFB SMEAR-POSITIVE — suspected active pulmonary TUBERCULOSIS; (B) a woman with profuse infectious DIARRHEA and a positive C. DIFFICILE toxin/PCR; (C) a young adult with fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, and PETECHIAE — suspected Neisseria MENINGITIDIS (meningococcal) meningitis; (D) a child with high fever, a maculopapular rash, and KOPLIK spots — MEASLES (rubeola); (E) a woman post-chemotherapy with an ANC of 300 (severe NEUTROPENIA) — needs PROTECTIVE environment; and (F) general patients requiring STANDARD precautions plus a PPE donning/doffing demonstration. The nurse must select the right precaution category, the right room (negative-pressure AIIR vs positive-pressure protective), the right PPE (N95 vs surgical mask), use soap-and-water/bleach for C. diff, assign immune staff to measles, and don/doff PPE in the correct sequence.
patient.room: Med-surg/step-down with access to AIRBORNE-INFECTION ISOLATION ROOMS (negative pressure) and a PROTECTIVE-ENVIRONMENT (positive-pressure/HEPA) room; PPE stocked at each door with correct signage; interdisciplinary team (provider, bedside RN, infection preventionist, environmental services); cohorting and dedicated equipment as needed

vitals.time: Patient A — suspected active pulmonary TB
vitals.temp: 38.2°C; cough × 4 weeks, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis; CXR cavitary upper-lobe lesion; sputum AFB SMEAR-POSITIVE → AIRBORNE precautions: N95 (fit-tested) respirator + AIIR NEGATIVE-pressure room, door CLOSED; patient wears a SURGICAL mask for any transport

vitals.time: Patient B — C. difficile infectious diarrhea
vitals.temp: 38.0°C; multiple watery stools, abdominal cramping; C. DIFFICILE toxin/PCR POSITIVE → CONTACT precautions (gown + gloves, private room/cohort, DEDICATED equipment); hand hygiene with SOAP AND WATER (alcohol does NOT kill spores); room cleaned with a SPORICIDAL/bleach agent

39.4°C; severe headache, photophobia, NUCHAL RIGIDITY, and a spreading PETECHIAL/purpuric rash — suspected Neisseria MENINGITIDIS → DROPLET precautions (surgical mask within ~3-6 ft, private room) for at least the first 24 h of effective antibiotics; clinically UNSTABLE — needs urgent antibiotics + close monitoring

39.0°C; high fever, cough/coryza/conjunctivitis, a descending maculopapular rash, and KOPLIK spots → AIRBORNE precautions (N95 + AIIR negative-pressure, door closed); assign IMMUNE staff — susceptible/nonimmune staff should NOT enter; patient masked for transport

36.9°C; post-chemo with ANC 300 (severe NEUTROPENIA) → PROTECTIVE ENVIRONMENT (reverse isolation): POSITIVE-pressure/HEPA room, door closed, NO fresh flowers/potted plants or standing water, only healthy visitors, meticulous hand hygiene — protects the patient FROM the environment (NOT negative pressure)

Standard precautions for ALL patients (hand hygiene, PPE per anticipated exposure, cough etiquette, safe injection, clean environment). PPE DON: gown → mask/respirator → goggles → gloves. PPE DOFF: gloves → goggles → gown → mask/respirator (most-contaminated first; respirator removed LAST, outside the room) with hand hygiene — signage posted, PPE stocked, cohorting/dedicated equipment as needed

labs.n: STANDARD precautions are the foundation for ALL patients
labs.v: STANDARD PRECAUTIONS apply to EVERY patient regardless of diagnosis and are the base layer onto which transmission-based precautions are added. They include HAND HYGIENE (before/after every patient contact), PPE selected by the ANTICIPATED EXPOSURE to blood/body fluids (gloves for contact with fluids/mucous membranes/nonintact skin; gown, mask, and eye protection if splash/spray is likely), RESPIRATORY HYGIENE/COUGH ETIQUETTE, SAFE INJECTION practices (one needle/syringe, one patient, one time), safe handling of contaminated equipment/linens, and a clean environment. Hand hygiene is the single most effective measure to prevent transmission. The nurse never relies on a patient's known diagnoses alone — standard precautions assume any patient may harbor a transmissible organism.
labs.r: STANDARD precautions = ALL patients, the foundation. Hand hygiene (most effective), PPE by anticipated exposure, cough etiquette, safe injections, clean equipment/environment. Transmission-based precautions are ADDED on top. RN role: apply standard precautions universally.

labs.n: CONTACT precautions — MDROs, C. difficile, norovirus, scabies, draining wounds (SOAP-AND-WATER for spores)
labs.v: CONTACT precautions are added for organisms spread by DIRECT/INDIRECT contact: MULTIDRUG-RESISTANT ORGANISMS (MRSA, VRE, CRE/ESBL), Clostridioides DIFFICILE, NOROVIRUS, SCABIES/lice, RSV, and major DRAINING wounds. Wear a GOWN and GLOVES for room entry; use a PRIVATE room or COHORT like-with-like; and use DEDICATED or single-use equipment (or disinfect between patients). CRITICAL: for C. DIFFICILE, NOROVIRUS, and SCABIES, perform hand hygiene with SOAP AND WATER — the mechanical action removes spores that ALCOHOL-BASED sanitizer does NOT kill — and clean the room with a SPORICIDAL agent (bleach). For Patient B (C. diff toxin positive), the nurse initiates contact precautions, gowns/gloves, uses dedicated equipment, washes with soap and water, and ensures bleach cleaning.
labs.r: CONTACT = MDROs (MRSA/VRE/CRE), C. diff, norovirus, scabies, RSV, draining wounds → GOWN + GLOVES, private/cohort, DEDICATED equipment. C. DIFF/NOROVIRUS/SCABIES → SOAP-AND-WATER (alcohol won't kill spores) + BLEACH cleaning. RN role: contact precautions + spore-appropriate hygiene.

labs.n: DROPLET precautions — influenza, pertussis, meningococcus, mumps/rubella, group A strep
labs.v: DROPLET precautions are added for organisms spread by LARGE respiratory droplets (which travel only a short distance and do not stay suspended): INFLUENZA, PERTUSSIS (whooping cough), Neisseria MENINGITIDIS (meningococcal disease), Haemophilus influenzae type b, MUMPS, RUBELLA, and group A streptococcal pharyngitis/scarlet fever. Wear a SURGICAL (procedure) mask when within ~3-6 feet of the patient (no special air handling is required because droplets fall quickly); use a PRIVATE room or cohort; keep the door able to remain open; and have the patient wear a SURGICAL MASK during transport. For Patient C (suspected meningococcal meningitis), the nurse initiates droplet precautions immediately and maintains them for at least the first 24 hours of effective antibiotics, while urgently facilitating antibiotic administration and monitoring the unstable patient.
labs.r: DROPLET = influenza, PERTUSSIS, Neisseria MENINGITIDIS, Hib, mumps/rubella, group A strep → SURGICAL MASK within ~3-6 ft, private/cohort, patient masked for transport. Meningococcus → droplet × first 24 h of effective antibiotics + urgent abx. RN role: droplet precautions + timely treatment.

labs.n: AIRBORNE precautions — TB, measles, varicella, disseminated zoster (N95 + AIIR negative pressure)
labs.v: AIRBORNE precautions are added for tiny airborne NUCLEI that remain suspended and travel on air currents: TUBERCULOSIS, MEASLES (rubeola), VARICELLA (chickenpox), and disseminated herpes ZOSTER (mnemonic 'My Chicken Hez TB'). Wear a FIT-TESTED N95 RESPIRATOR (or PAPR) — a surgical mask is NOT adequate; place the patient in an AIRBORNE-INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM (AIIR) with NEGATIVE PRESSURE relative to the hallway, ≥6-12 AIR CHANGES per hour, and the DOOR KEPT CLOSED; and have the patient wear a SURGICAL mask (not an N95) during any necessary transport. For MEASLES and VARICELLA, SUSCEPTIBLE (nonimmune) staff should NOT enter — assign IMMUNE staff. Patient A (AFB smear-positive TB) and Patient D (measles) both require airborne precautions/AIIR; varicella and disseminated zoster also require CONTACT precautions in addition (combine routes). The nurse initiates airborne precautions on clinical suspicion without waiting for confirmation.
labs.r: AIRBORNE = TB, MEASLES, VARICELLA, disseminated ZOSTER → fit-tested N95/PAPR (NOT surgical mask) + AIIR NEGATIVE pressure, ≥6-12 ACH, door CLOSED; patient wears SURGICAL mask for transport. MEASLES/VARICELLA → assign IMMUNE staff. Varicella/zoster also need CONTACT. Initiate on suspicion. RN role: airborne precautions + N95 + AIIR.

labs.n: PROTECTIVE environment + PPE don/doff + nursing role; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PROTECTIVE ENVIRONMENT (reverse/neutropenic isolation) has the OPPOSITE goal — protecting a SEVERELY IMMUNOCOMPROMISED patient (neutropenic chemo, hematopoietic stem-cell transplant) FROM the environment: a POSITIVE-PRESSURE room with HEPA filtration and high air exchange, door closed, NO fresh flowers/potted plants or standing water (fungal/Pseudomonas risk), only healthy visitors (no one with infection), and minimized exposure. It is NOT negative pressure (that is for airborne containment). PPE SEQUENCE: DON gown → mask/respirator → goggles → gloves (before entering); DOFF gloves → goggles → gown → mask/respirator (most contaminated first; remove the respirator LAST, after leaving the room/closing the door) with HAND HYGIENE after removal. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT use a surgical mask for TB/measles (N95 required); did NOT use alcohol sanitizer alone for C. diff (soap-and-water needed); did NOT place the neutropenic patient in NEGATIVE pressure (needs positive); did NOT delay precautions awaiting culture confirmation; and did NOT assign susceptible staff to measles/varicella. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: standard precautions for all; correct route identification; right room (negative for airborne, positive for protective) and PPE (N95 vs surgical mask); soap-and-water + bleach for spores; immune-staff assignment; correct don/doff; cohorting + dedicated equipment; signage/stocked PPE; masked transport; and nonstigmatizing patient/family teaching. EXPECTED outcome: each patient is correctly isolated, transmission is prevented, the immunocompromised patient is protected, and staff/visitors use PPE correctly. NURSING ROLE: recognize the route from the cues; initiate the correct precautions; choose the right room and PPE; use spore-appropriate hygiene; assign immune staff; don/doff safely; and educate.
labs.r: PROTECTIVE = neutropenic/HSCT → POSITIVE-pressure/HEPA, no plants/standing water, healthy visitors only (protects PATIENT, not negative pressure). PPE DON gown→mask→goggles→gloves; DOFF gloves→goggles→gown→mask (respirator last) + hand hygiene. NEGATIVES: no surgical mask for TB/measles, no alcohol-only for C. diff, no negative pressure for neutropenic, no delaying precautions, no susceptible staff for measles. PROTECTIVE: right route/room/PPE, soap+bleach, immune staff, don/doff, cohort, teaching. RN role: select + initiate + monitor + educate.

labs.n: Patient A — sputum AFB smear + chest imaging
labs.v: Sputum AFB smear POSITIVE with a cavitary upper-lobe infiltrate on chest x-ray, in a patient with chronic cough, night sweats, weight loss, and hemoptysis — strongly consistent with active pulmonary tuberculosis (NAA/culture pending). Mandates immediate AIRBORNE precautions (N95 + AIIR negative-pressure) and public-health notification.
labs.r: AFB smear POSITIVE + cavitary CXR = active pulmonary TB until proven otherwise → AIRBORNE (N95 + AIIR) + report. RN role: contain airborne spread immediately.

labs.n: Patient B — stool C. difficile toxin/PCR + WBC
labs.v: Stool C. difficile toxin/PCR POSITIVE with leukocytosis (WBC elevated) and profuse diarrhea — confirms C. difficile infection. Requires CONTACT precautions, SOAP-AND-WATER hand hygiene (alcohol ineffective against spores), dedicated equipment, and sporicidal/bleach environmental cleaning.
labs.r: C. diff toxin/PCR POSITIVE → CONTACT + SOAP-AND-WATER + bleach + dedicated equipment. RN role: spore-appropriate contact precautions.

labs.v: Lumbar puncture shows cloudy CSF with markedly elevated WBC (neutrophilic), low glucose, high protein, and gram-NEGATIVE diplococci — consistent with Neisseria meningitidis; with fever, nuchal rigidity, and a spreading petechial/purpuric rash and hypotension, this is a medical emergency. Requires immediate effective antibiotics and DROPLET precautions for the first 24 hours of therapy.
labs.r: CSF gram-negative diplococci + petechiae + nuchal rigidity = meningococcal meningitis (emergency) → urgent antibiotics + DROPLET precautions × first 24 h. RN role: droplet precautions + rapid treatment of an unstable patient.

labs.v: ANC 300/µL — SEVERE NEUTROPENIA (ANC <500) post-chemotherapy, placing the patient at high risk of life-threatening infection (fever may be the only sign). Requires a PROTECTIVE ENVIRONMENT (positive-pressure/HEPA, no plants/standing water, healthy visitors only) and prompt evaluation/empiric antibiotics if fever develops.
labs.r: ANC 300 = severe NEUTROPENIA → PROTECTIVE environment (positive pressure/HEPA, no plants, healthy visitors); fever = emergency. RN role: protect the immunocompromised patient FROM the environment.

labs.v: Audit confirms correct practice: signage posted and PPE stocked at each door; staff donning/doffing in the correct sequence with hand hygiene; soap-and-water and bleach used for the C. diff room; N95 fit-tested staff and a closed-door negative-pressure room for the airborne patients with immune staff assigned to measles; positive-pressure room and no plants for the neutropenic patient; cohorting and dedicated equipment where private rooms were limited.
labs.r: Audit: signage/PPE stocked, correct don/doff + hand hygiene, soap+bleach for C. diff, N95 + negative-pressure + immune staff for airborne, positive-pressure/no-plants for neutropenic, cohort + dedicated equipment. RN role: verify correct precaution implementation.

nursesNote: Med-surg/step-down — RN assigning ISOLATION (transmission-based) precautions across new patients. (A) cough × 4 wk, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis, cavitary CXR, sputum AFB SMEAR-POSITIVE → suspected active pulmonary TB → AIRBORNE: fit-tested N95 + AIIR NEGATIVE-pressure, door CLOSED, patient SURGICAL mask for transport; report to public health. (B) profuse diarrhea, C. DIFFICILE toxin/PCR positive, WBC up → CONTACT: gown+gloves, private/cohort, DEDICATED equipment, SOAP-AND-WATER hand hygiene (alcohol does NOT kill spores), BLEACH/sporicidal cleaning. (C) fever, nuchal rigidity, PETECHIAE, hypotension, CSF gram-negative diplococci → suspected Neisseria MENINGITIDIS (emergency) → DROPLET: surgical mask within ~3-6 ft, private room, × first 24 h effective antibiotics + URGENT abx + monitoring (UNSTABLE). (D) high fever, maculopapular rash, KOPLIK spots → MEASLES → AIRBORNE: N95 + AIIR negative-pressure; assign IMMUNE staff (susceptible staff do NOT enter); patient masked for transport. (E) post-chemo ANC 300 (severe NEUTROPENIA) → PROTECTIVE ENVIRONMENT: POSITIVE-pressure/HEPA, door closed, NO flowers/plants/standing water, healthy visitors only — protects the PATIENT (NOT negative pressure). STANDARD precautions for ALL (hand hygiene = most effective, PPE by anticipated exposure, cough etiquette, safe injections). PPE DON gown→mask/respirator→goggles→gloves; DOFF gloves→goggles→gown→mask/respirator (most contaminated first, respirator LAST outside room) + hand hygiene. Cohort like-with-like, dedicated equipment, signage + PPE stocked at doors, masked transport only as needed. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no surgical mask for TB/measles (N95), no alcohol-only for C. diff (soap+water), no negative pressure for neutropenic (positive), no delaying precautions for confirmation, no susceptible staff for measles/varicella. Note: varicella/disseminated zoster need AIRBORNE + CONTACT combined. Outcome: each patient correctly isolated, transmission prevented, immunocompromised patient protected, PPE used correctly.
providerOrders: ADMIT/ASSIGN: place each patient on the correct transmission-based precautions in addition to standard precautions

PATIENT A (suspected active pulmonary TB): AIRBORNE precautions — fit-tested N95/PAPR, AIIR negative-pressure room with door closed; surgical mask on patient for transport; sputum AFB ×3/NAA + culture; notify public health/infection prevention
PATIENT B (C. difficile positive): CONTACT precautions — gown + gloves, private room/cohort, dedicated equipment; hand hygiene with SOAP AND WATER; sporicidal (bleach) environmental cleaning

PATIENT C (suspected meningococcal meningitis): DROPLET precautions — surgical mask within ~3-6 ft, private room; maintain ≥24 h after effective antibiotics begin; administer empiric antibiotics emergently; monitor closely (unstable)
PATIENT D (measles/rubeola): AIRBORNE precautions — N95 + AIIR negative-pressure, door closed; assign IMMUNE staff only; surgical mask on patient for transport; notify public health

PATIENT E (neutropenic, ANC 300): PROTECTIVE environment — positive-pressure/HEPA room, door closed; NO fresh flowers/potted plants or standing water; only healthy visitors; neutropenic precautions; treat fever as an emergency (cultures + empiric antibiotics)
PPE: don gown → mask/respirator → goggles → gloves; doff gloves → goggles → gown → mask/respirator with hand hygiene; remove the respirator last, outside the room
GENERAL: cohort like-with-like when private rooms are limited; post correct signage and stock PPE at each door; perform hand hygiene before/after every contact; minimize and mask transport
EDUCATION: teach the patient, family, and visitors the reason for precautions and correct PPE/hand hygiene without stigmatizing the patient

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this isolation assignment?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Patient A's AFB smear-positive cavitary TB requires airborne precautions — a fit-tested N95 and a negative-pressure AIIR with the door closed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — TB is airborne; N95 + negative-pressure AIIR are required (a surgical mask is inadequate). RN role: contain airborne spread.

opts.text: Patient B's C. difficile requires contact precautions with SOAP-AND-WATER hand hygiene and bleach cleaning, because alcohol does not kill spores
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — C. diff spores survive alcohol; soap-and-water plus a sporicidal agent are essential. RN role: spore-appropriate hygiene.

opts.text: Patient C's suspected meningococcal meningitis requires droplet precautions plus urgent antibiotics, and the patient is clinically unstable
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — meningococcus is droplet-spread and an emergency; precautions plus rapid treatment are needed. RN role: droplet precautions + timely care.

opts.text: Patient D's measles requires airborne precautions, and susceptible (nonimmune) staff should not enter — assign immune staff
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — measles is airborne and highly contagious; immune staff are assigned. RN role: airborne precautions + immune staffing.

opts.text: Patient E's neutropenia (ANC 300) requires a protective environment — a POSITIVE-pressure room, no plants/standing water, and only healthy visitors
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — protective environment shields the immunocompromised patient (positive pressure, not negative). RN role: protect the patient from the environment.

opts.text: Standard precautions (hand hygiene, PPE by anticipated exposure) apply to every patient regardless of diagnosis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — standard precautions are the universal foundation. RN role: apply them to all.

opts.text: An alcohol-based hand sanitizer is sufficient after caring for the C. difficile patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — alcohol does NOT kill C. difficile spores; hand hygiene must be soap and water with mechanical washing.

opts.text: Place the neutropenic patient in a negative-pressure room for protection
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG and backwards — a protective environment uses POSITIVE pressure to keep contaminated air out; negative pressure is for airborne containment.

opts.text: Airborne precautions (TB, measles, varicella, disseminated zoster) require a fit-tested N95/PAPR and a negative-pressure AIIR with the door closed
opts.rat: CORRECT — airborne route and its room/PPE requirements. Foundation for containment.

opts.text: Droplet precautions (influenza, pertussis, meningococcus, mumps/rubella, group A strep) require a surgical mask within ~3-6 feet; no special air handling is needed
opts.rat: CORRECT — droplets fall quickly; a surgical mask suffices. Foundation for droplet care.

opts.text: Contact precautions (MDROs, C. diff, norovirus, scabies, draining wounds) require gown and gloves and dedicated equipment; C. diff/norovirus need soap-and-water and bleach
opts.rat: CORRECT — contact route, PPE, and the spore exception. Foundation for contact care.

opts.text: A protective environment protects the immunocompromised patient with positive pressure/HEPA, no plants or standing water, and only healthy visitors
opts.rat: CORRECT — reverse/neutropenic isolation principles. Foundation for protecting the host.

opts.text: PPE is donned gown → mask/respirator → goggles → gloves and doffed gloves → goggles → gown → mask/respirator with hand hygiene (most contaminated removed first)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the don/doff sequences. Foundation for not self-contaminating.

opts.text: Precautions are initiated on clinical suspicion and not delayed while awaiting culture confirmation; combine routes when needed (e.g., varicella = airborne + contact)
opts.rat: CORRECT — early initiation and combined precautions. Foundation for timely, complete protection.

opts.text: A surgical mask provides adequate respiratory protection when entering a TB or measles patient's room
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — airborne organisms require a fit-tested N95/PAPR; a surgical mask is insufficient.

opts.text: A neutropenic protective environment uses negative pressure, the same as an airborne isolation room
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — protective environments use POSITIVE pressure to keep contaminants out; negative pressure would expose the patient.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank the order to address these patients/tasks
steps.inst: Rank from #1 (address FIRST) to #5 (LAST), weighing patient acuity and transmission risk.

opts.text: Patient C — suspected meningococcal meningitis: clinically unstable; initiate droplet precautions and facilitate emergent antibiotics
opts.rat: #1 — The unstable, time-critical patient (meningococcemia is rapidly fatal) needs droplet precautions AND immediate treatment. RN role: precautions + emergent care.

opts.text: Patient A — AFB smear-positive TB: place in a negative-pressure AIIR with N95 to contain active airborne transmission
opts.rat: #2 — Active, smear-positive TB poses a high airborne transmission risk that must be contained promptly. RN role: airborne containment.

opts.text: Patient D — measles: place in a negative-pressure AIIR with N95 and assign immune staff (highly contagious airborne)
opts.rat: #3 — Measles is extremely contagious and airborne; rapid isolation with immune staff limits spread. RN role: airborne isolation + immune staffing.

opts.text: Patient B — C. difficile: initiate contact precautions with soap-and-water hygiene and bleach cleaning
opts.rat: #4 — Important contact-spread organism, but lower immediate acuity/airborne risk than the above. RN role: contact + spore-appropriate hygiene.

opts.text: Patient E — neutropenic: establish the protective (positive-pressure) environment to shield the patient
opts.rat: #5 — Essential, but this protects one stable patient from the environment rather than preventing spread from a contagious source. RN role: protective environment.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Match each patient to the required precaution
steps.inst: Classify the transmission-based precaution each condition requires: CONTACT, DROPLET, or AIRBORNE.

opts.text: MRSA-colonized surgical wound with drainage
opts.rat: MDROs spread by contact → gown + gloves, dedicated equipment, private/cohort.

opts.text: Clostridioides difficile infectious diarrhea
opts.rat: Contact precautions PLUS soap-and-water hygiene and bleach cleaning (alcohol does not kill spores).

opts.text: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) in stool
opts.rat: MDRO spread by contact → gown + gloves, dedicated equipment.

opts.text: Active pulmonary tuberculosis (AFB smear-positive)
opts.rat: Airborne nuclei → N95 + negative-pressure AIIR, door closed.

opts.text: Measles (rubeola)
opts.rat: Highly contagious airborne → N95 + AIIR; assign immune staff.

opts.text: Varicella (chickenpox) / disseminated herpes zoster
opts.rat: Airborne (AIIR + N95) — and ALSO add contact precautions for the vesicular lesions; the room requirement is airborne.

opts.text: Pertussis (whooping cough)
opts.rat: Large-droplet spread → surgical mask within ~3-6 ft, private/cohort.

opts.text: Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcal) meningitis
opts.rat: Droplet precautions for the first 24 h of effective antibiotics → surgical mask within ~3-6 ft.

opts.text: Seasonal influenza
opts.rat: Droplet spread → surgical mask within ~3-6 ft, private/cohort.

opts.text: Mumps
opts.rat: Droplet spread → surgical mask within ~3-6 ft, private/cohort.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence safe isolation setup and care
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions for a patient requiring isolation. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and identify the required precaution type from the diagnosis/cues (initiate on suspicion — don't wait for confirmation)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Recognizing the route and starting hand hygiene drives every subsequent choice. RN role: recognize + initiate.

opts.text: Place the patient in the correct room (negative-pressure AIIR for airborne, positive-pressure for protective, private/cohort for contact/droplet), post signage, and stock PPE
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The right room and supplies must be ready before care. RN role: correct room + signage + PPE.

opts.text: Don PPE in the correct sequence before entering (gown → mask/respirator → goggles → gloves)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Proper donning before entry prevents exposure. RN role: don PPE correctly.

opts.text: Provide care with dedicated equipment and appropriate hand hygiene (soap-and-water for C. diff), then doff PPE in sequence (gloves → goggles → gown → mask/respirator) with hand hygiene
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Safe care and correct doffing prevent self-contamination and spread. RN role: dedicated equipment + safe doffing.

opts.text: Educate the patient, family, and visitors; arrange masked transport only when necessary; and document
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Teaching and controlled transport sustain the precautions. RN role: educate + document.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse placed the AFB smear-positive TB patient in a negative-pressure AIIR and wore a fit-tested N95 with the door closed
opts.rat: Correct airborne precautions for TB. Correct practice.

opts.text: After caring for the C. difficile patient, the nurse washed with soap and water and ensured bleach cleaning of the room
opts.rat: Spores require mechanical washing and a sporicidal agent. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse initiated droplet precautions and facilitated emergent antibiotics for the suspected meningococcal meningitis patient
opts.rat: Droplet precautions plus rapid treatment are correct for meningococcemia. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse assigned an immune (vaccinated/seropositive) staff member to the measles patient and used an N95 in a negative-pressure room
opts.rat: Immune staffing and airborne precautions are correct for measles. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse placed the neutropenic patient in a positive-pressure room and removed fresh flowers and potted plants
opts.rat: Protective environment shields the immunocompromised patient. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse wore only a surgical mask to enter the active TB patient's room
opts.rat: ⚠️ Airborne precautions require a fit-tested N95/PAPR; a surgical mask is inadequate. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse used alcohol-based sanitizer alone after caring for the C. difficile patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ Alcohol does not kill C. diff spores; soap-and-water washing is required. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse placed the neutropenic patient in a negative-pressure room 'to keep germs out'
opts.rat: ⚠️ Backwards — negative pressure pulls air (and contaminants) in; a protective environment needs POSITIVE pressure. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse delayed all precautions for the suspected TB and meningitis patients until cultures returned
opts.rat: ⚠️ Precautions are initiated on clinical suspicion; delaying allows transmission. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse removed the N95 respirator inside the room before exiting and doffed gloves last
opts.rat: ⚠️ The respirator is removed LAST, after leaving the room/closing the door; gloves (most contaminated) are removed FIRST. Improper practice.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
